posts.text: 58% of Canadian employees are emotionally burnt out. And the reason might surprise you. It's not just workload. It's financial stress doing the damage. A new Harris & Partners survey of 12,000+ Canadians found that 53% saw their workload increase without a pay bump. 45% picked up a second job or gig just to make ends meet. And 76% said job or financial stress hurt their mental health in the past year. Burnout isn't just a wellness problem anymore. It's a financial security problem. That's why comprehensive employee benefits: mental health support, financial planning tools, and strong pension plans, matter more than ever. When people feel secure, they can actually recover. What is your organization doing to close that gap?

Are Canadians spending less time in retirement than they used to? The data says yes. The average Canadian retirement period peaked at 22.7 years in 2012 and has been shrinking ever since. As of 2025, it sits at just 20.5 years. Here's the story behind the numbers: In 1977, the average Canadian retired at 65.5 and spent about 15.9 years in retirement. Over the following two decades, retirement ages fell steadily, hitting a low of 61.4 in 1999 and with rising life expectancy, the retirement window kept growing. But since 2012, the trend has reversed. We're retiring later, and the shrinking worker-to-retiree ratio means that shift is likely to continue.

This week is Mental Health Week in Canada (May 4–10), and the numbers are a call to action for every employer: 📊 42% of Canadian employees rate their mental health as fair or poor 📊 1 in 4 employees have considered leaving their jobs due to mental health challenges 📊 Employers that invest in mental health programs see $4 back for every $1 spent. This year's CMHA theme: Stronger Connections = Better Mental Health, is a reminder that the support we build into our workplaces matters more than we think. At CU Trust, mental wellness isn't a checkbox. It's part of how we think about benefits, pension security, and whole-person wellbeing. 📩 We've included a curated set of mental health resources and tools in our newsletters to employers, practical guidance your teams can start using right now.

Do you know what kind of pension plan you have?

A study by IG Wealth Management found that 1 in 4 Canadians with an employer pension doesn't know whether it's a Defined Benefit or Defined Contribution plan. And that difference matters a lot when you're planning for retirement.

Here's a simple breakdown:
🔵 Defined Benefit (DB) — Your employer promises you a set monthly income in retirement, regardless of market conditions. You know exactly what you'll receive.
🟢 Defined Contribution (DC) — You and your employer both contribute to your account, but your retirement income depends on how those investments perform over time.

Our ask this week: Pull out your latest pension statement and take 10 minutes to review it. Look for your plan type, your contribution rate (if applicable), and your projected benefit or account balance.

Have questions about your CU Trust pension? Reach out to us directly at pensions@cutrust.com.

💬 Did you know what type of pension you had before reading this?
#RetirementPlanning #EmployeeBenefits #PensionAwareness #WorkplaceWellness #PersonalFinance

This week marks Canadian Fertility Awareness Week (April 19–25) — a reminder that reproductive health and hormonal wellbeing are an integral part of overall quality of life. For many employees and their partners, questions around fertility and family planning can bring both hope and uncertainty, and often intersect with stress, mental health, and day-to-day well-being. These are quiet realities that shape how people show up at work. At CU Trust, we believe a strong benefits plan should meet members where they are in life. Through our Family Building Benefits, eligible plan members and their families have access to support for fertility planning and related needs. To our participating credit union employers: thank you for making these benefits available to your teams. Supportive, inclusive coverage helps our sector continue to lead with care. #CanadianFertilityAwarenessWeek #EmployeeBenefits #CreditUnions #WorkplaceWellbeing
